sentences.text: Well, it was a hell of a difference.
Because Vilna was a old, established Jewish center with hundreds of synagogues.
In one place in the shul [NON-ENGLISH], they had more than 10 synagogues.
While in my hometown, there was only one.
It was a new town.
And it had only one synagogue, and before it, a little [NON-ENGLISH].
But the good part was that there was a very intensive life around the synagogue.

As an example, every day, the [HEBREW]-- that means the-- the group that studied Talmud-- that included my late father and myself-- used to meet, especially in the summer, around 6pm, for one hour, to study Talmud, together with the rabbi.
And the rabbi was [INAUDIBLE] Zeev Mamyoffe.
Would you spell that, please.
Zeev.
Z-E-E-V.
Mamyoffe.
M-A-M-Y-O-double-F-E.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were about 60, 70 participants.

Considering that the whole town had about two-- 200 Jewish families, it was a very high percentage of Jews educated in Talmud, who could participate in the [HEBREW], in the daily portion of the Talmud.
And from this point of view, it was probably one of the better organized towns-- small towns in-- in-- in Lithuania.
And Jewish life, of course, centered around the synagogue.
I attended in the shul [NON-ENGLISH] And I once I had the privilege of davening [HEBREW] with the Vilna [INAUDIBLE], Haim Ozer Grodzenski.
Haim Ozer Grodzenski.
H-A-I-M O-Z-E-R G-R-O-D-Z-E-N-S-K-I.
Grodzenski.
Before he passed away.
Reb ?] Haim Ozer.
The Vilna [INAUDIBLE].
It was a [INAUDIBLE].
Not the old Vilna [INAUDIBLE] that was 200 years ago, but the present [INAUDIBLE].
Well, it was-- in-- in my hometown, they were not all religiously oriented.
But-- and those that were were mostly modern.
You didn't have any ultra-Orthodox.
You didn't have any Hasidim.
You didn't have [HEBREW].
You didn't have [HEBREW].
You didn't have that.
It didn't exist in Lithuania.
But in Vilna, you had a great following of Orthodoxy.
You had a great following of Zionism and a great following for Jewish socialist Bundists as well.
Not at all.
Not at all.
We-- we regarded them as [INAUDIBLE].
Why?
Because they were not for the rebuilding of Jewish life in Palestine, which was our aim.
Yes.
I did.
I did.
I did.
And I was very proud of it.
It was part of Betar.
And also Maccabee.
No.
In Vilna, it was different.
Because in Vilna, when I came to Vilna, part of it was-- because it was after the Hitler-Stalin agreement, the falling apart of Poland.
And when the Soviets succeeded in forcing the Baltic states into mutual defense agreements and stationing in every Baltic country, four or five bases of soldiers in each country.
So, we knew that Lithuania was living, actually, on-- on borrowed time.
And one of my reasons to going to Vilna was not only to seek higher education, of course, which I did.

But also, in order to disappear in the big crowd, so as not to-- because I believed that one day, probably sooner than later, the Soviets will occupy Lithuania, and then they will go after the Zionists.
Mm-hmm.
Which they did.
I was too young and was a small fry.
But I know how many others were expelled to Siberia.
Well, we knew all that anybody else knew.
Because we used to get, every day, three or four daily newspapers.

We also used to-- I belonged to a group, also, and the attorney [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], we were-- and the-- our teacher, [NON-ENGLISH]-- we were interested in the study of political developments.
It was a private group.
And we used to get also foreign newspapers, including Berliner Tageblatt, and [NON-ENGLISH], and-- and-- and others.
And we knew exactly what everybody else knew about Nazism.

Nobody knew at that time the final solution aims of Hitler, which were formulated, I believe, in [NON-ENGLISH] in 1942, after Hitler noticed from the Evian conference that no country, including the United States, wants the Jews.
So he decided that Jews are sort of fair game.
Especially when the ship St.
Louis returned-- was-- went around the world, came back to-- so we knew all about it.
We knew already in '39, and of course, it was-- I don't know when the ship was-- was going.
But we knew all that was happening.
We knew about camps in Germany.
Because while there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a control of the press and so on, but some news were always printed about camps in Germany.
And you must realize that Lithuania and Germany were not on the best terms, because of the dispute over Memel, over Klaipeda.
So sometimes they used to print in the Yiddish papers, but also the Lithuanian papers, about the atrocities, and about the-- so on and [INAUDIBLE] the Kristallnacht.
And others.
So we knew all about it.
Well, we knew of Stalin that he was a dictator of-- of Russia.
And most of what we knew was negative.
Except that in-- in '39 and 1940, when Poland fell apart, and Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia tried to keep their neutrality, and everybody knew that they will not be able to.
And the rest of them-- the [INAUDIBLE] were far apart and unable to help.
So then we knew that it would be either Hitler would occupy, or Russia.
So we-- we said, we rather be red than dead.
Well, we did not know everything because there was a-- there were secret clauses.
And one of the secret clauses was the division of the Baltic countries.
Which are the-- on the first-- in the first agreement, they're supposed to be divided, Latvia and Estonia to the Soviets, Lithuania to Nazi Germany.
After a-- a week, Stalin changed his mind and he renegotiated the treaty.
And according to the new agreement, the-- Lithuania was also to be ceded to Russia.
Well, it was mixed feelings.
But many people, at the first gut feeling, were happy that it was the Russians and not the Germans.
I speak of the Jewish population.
The Lithuanians, of course, grieved.
For them, it was the loss of their independence they-- they have regained from the czar after 127 years of Russian occupation of Lithuania.
So for them, it was a tragedy.
And they viewed the throwing of flowers to the Russian soldiers and the approval by some people as treachery, from their point of view, as ungratefulness from the Jewish community.
Now, afterwards, of course, the Jews started to feel what Russia and what Russian rule really meant.
So it was a total change.
All the Hebrew schools were closed and made into Yiddish schools, state schools, or closed completely.
All the Jewish organizations, except the Communist Party-- the small, minute Communist Party of Lithuania, which had 900 members, and 2/3 of them Jews.

All other organizations, like the labor Zionists, the [INAUDIBLE] Zionists, the-- the religious Zionists, the religious [INAUDIBLE] that was not Zionist, the Betar, revisionists-- all of them were declared illegal.
And their leadership was hunted.
All the businesses were nationalized-- the bigger ones.
The-- the fabrics, the factories, all industrial enterprises, 80% owned by Jews, were nationalized.
All the bank accounts and the bank safes were nationalized-- again, 80%.
And the banks themselves were all-- 80% of it was Jewish.

So what I would say is that the Soviets managed to break the economic back of the Jewish community, render them penniless, so it was easier for Hitler and-- to do what he did, with the help of local Lithuanians, collaborators.
Because they were, in many ways, robbed of their funds to resist, or to-- to bribe, or to, whatever, you know?
Well, they-- they started to-- changing courses, especially in economics.
They added courses of Marxism and Leninism.
But it took them a long time.
Because Lithuanians are very-- even the-- the so-called Communists were, in fact, Lithuanian nationalists at heart.
So everything went much slower than expected.
Or not to the same extent as in Latvia and Estonia.
I don't think I [INAUDIBLE].
But I think by the time we had to take the courses, the war broke out or something, or I went to hospital.
But there were some courses.
I know, especially-- I think there was, yes.
I think I did take a-- a short course.
Which, of course, we all made fun of.
Nobody took it seriously.
Well, you didn't make it in front of the-- of the professor, but among ourselves.
Well, it took them awhile to get around to it.
After two, three months, they said, well-- or maybe even earlier-- they said, well, Lithuania is now part of the happy family of Soviet Republics.
That means you don't need a-- a passport department.
Because passports are given by the minister of internal affairs.
They [INAUDIBLE] that.
They transferred us.
The small fry were transferred to the Communal Properties Department.
And I worked in the Communal Properties Department.
And that's where I had this situation with Dainelis and Bukauskas.
OK.
As I was sitting-- I will come to it.
As I was sitting one day in my office in the Communal Properties, a total stranger came into my room and said, you don't know me, but I knew your parents back home in Mazeikiai.
I was with the Lithuanian State Security.
And now, I and my friend, Bukauskas, are on the run.
And we are afraid to-- to hang around without jobs because we are liable to be arrested and sent to Siberia.
What can you do for us?
So I was, of course, young, was 19.
And my instincts were good.
On the other hand, I was afraid lest these people might entrap me.
Maybe they are turncoats and just they are trying to see whether I would be able and willing to help the enemies of the Soviet Union.
The whole thing took about three minutes.
And I decided, yes, I'm going to help them.
And I sent them to the other room where they received jobs as home-- house managers.
Because there were many nationalized homes, the big homes in Vilna, were nationalized.
And they need-- they needed managers.
So I send them there and they got a jobs.
And one of them, Dainelis, told me that he was very grateful and he knew that I would not disappointed him.
He gave me his phone number in case I ever want to get in touch with him.
Of course, this was the last thing on my mind.
But it so happened that a few months later-- Mm-hmm.
--I certainly needed his telephone.
Would you spell his name and the other gentleman you helped?
Dainelis.
D-A-I-N-E-L-I-S.
His first name is A-- Antanas.
A-N-T-A-N-A-S.
Antanas Dainelis.
And I believe, but I'm not so sure, [NON-ENGLISH] Bukauskas, I believe.
But I'm not sure of the first name, so I'll just give you the second name.
Fine.
Bukauskas, whom I did not know at all.
B-U-K-A-U-S-K-A-S.
No.
No.
But I knew that they were working.
They were working because the office of the Housing Department was very close to me.
It's actually two doors away.
We knew a little.
In the German part?
We knew a little.
I-- we-- I-- we didn't know in detail.
But we knew a little.
Whatever came through the neutral countries that were there.
And thought that was not too much that-- came-- came-- came out.
Maybe those that were older than I and that were professionally interested, you know?
Maybe they knew more.
We knew a little.
We knew that it was bad and that Jews were persecuted, and some Jews were sent back and forth across the-- the boundary once or twice.
This I knew.
Yes.
I-- I certainly had.
First, in my capacity as an officer of the passport department--
When they came in, many of them, I screened them and I--
--gave them papers.
And the-- afterwards, some of them were young people, university students, that I met at university.
With some of them, I even continued my friendship in Israel afterwards.
Oh, we knew plenty.
Because we knew from history, Lithuania being on the-- at the crossroads between east and west, we knew all about [NON-ENGLISH], the push towards the east.
Because the Germans wanted some [NON-ENGLISH]-- they needed more space.
And they wanted to subject the Slav countries, mainly.
That is, Poland and Russia, use their people that they regarded as subhumans--
--destroy the Jews and the-- and the gypsies, and-- and create more space for-- for-- for Germans.
No.
Not we.
Maybe there were some with-- with really elaborate radios, but--
Well, we-- we knew, of course, Great Britain enjoyed, in the Jewish community, and the-- a very high standard of-- how shall I say-- a-- a-- a very high [INAUDIBLE].
They-- they were-- said, oh, as British Empire, you know, a quarter of the world belongs to them.
The sun never sets on the British Empire.
And we thought, also, the-- the British character, you know--
--will somehow, hopefully, prevail.
But, we were worried because we see-- we saw what Hitler did, and how easily he destroyed the French army for the second time.
The same way, by circumventing the Maginot Line and going through Belgium and Holland.
So, we were very worried.
Not yet.
Because Lithuania was a rich country.
Farmers were very productive.
There was no shortage of any kind.
On the contrary, in the first few months, there was a kind of a little boom.
Because people were worried about what might happen, and because they-- we knew the jokes about, you know, the lack of matches the lack of other things in the Soviet Union.
So people started to hoard, to buy more.
Hoarding up.
So those that had goods could sell them at better prices.
And there was a while-- a while-- that it created even a little boom.
But afterwards, when the-- the Russian officers started to grab everything, and there was no replacement, and there was a shortage, then, of course, prices jacked up.
Did you ever have any direct contact with the Russian soldiers during the Russian occupation of Vilna?
You mean in 1940?
From 19-- from when they come in, in the fall of '39, until June of '41.

There-- there-- there was a, I believe, there was a-- a fellow called [NON-ENGLISH], Lieutenant Colonel, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the military commander of Vilna-- who apparently was a son of Russian nobility.
Officers, and-- who probably did not tell, or somehow decided, probably in-- in 19-- in the early '20s, maybe, to throw in his lot with the Russians.
And he and his wife were very gentle and very nice and very well-educated.
On the other hand, I met some that were quite anti-Semitic.
Yes.
A-- one-- one Russian officer, when he heard my name, Trigubov-- which-- which means, in Russian-- I don't even know how we got the-- the name Trigubov-- it means the man of the three lips.
Three [RUSSIAN].
So when he heard it, he thought that I was a Russian.
And he was very happy.
And when I told him, [NON-ENGLISH]-- I am a Jew-- he said, how dare you to call yourself such a Russian aristocracy name?
Or a Siberian aristocracy name.
So I said, well, I-- I don't know how I got the name.
But we were always called Trigubov.
And I didn't know that you-- as a Communist-- are interested in nobility, that there was a name of nobility-- well, I really, I think that's beside the point.
And he said, no, no, no.
He says, it's not beside the point.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH].
I am a Communist, but I am also Russian.
So he was quite-- quite nationalistic.
I don't think so.
Unless he made use of it.
I don't think he did.
Because, well, for him it was just another Jew who calls himself a Trigubov.
I don't think so.
Yes.
Well, we saw that Hitler was going perfectly unopposed.
I mean, he was very successful.
And we couldn't see how he could avoid going especially [? to-- ?] to Lithuania, which was on his way to Moscow-- I mean, to [NON-ENGLISH] and Moscow.
And we believed that-- that that will be, actually, the Russian aim-- the-- excuse me-- the German aim.
The Lithuanian government?
The-- the--
Well, the Lithuanian government was already a Soviet government at that time.
And the-- the Soviets, well, on one hand, they expect it.
And that's why they said, at that time, I mean, secretly, to Communists, that we signed the agreement with Hitler because we are not ready yet for war.
They-- they certainly did prepare for war.
But at the same time, there was a great deal of negligence.
And I was told that the day when Hitler attacked, most Soviet aircraft were unprepared for takeoff because they-- they had-- there was no gasoline.
They were-- had-- they had to be inspected.
And it could have very well be a German [INAUDIBLE] trick.
But this, I'm not so sure.
And that-- I'm-- I also not so sure whether I knew it then or I just know it now.
You see, I was in Vilna, studying.
And all of a sudden, I received a-- a letter from my father, that he-- he has to talk to me about a very important subject.
The subject was that he was contacted in our-- our hometown, Mazeikiai, by a [?
Lithuanized ?] Russian, Tominov, the son of the letter carrier.
And the man said, Mr.
Trigubov, you have family in Russia.
I know, because you receive letters.
His father was the letter carrier.
Said, why don't you take your family and go to Russia?
Because this is dangerous, to stay here.
So my father said, why should I go from my home, again, back to Russia?
And he said, because we are very well organized here, the Lithuanians.
And war is imminent.
And when the war starts, many people will suffer-- the innocent together with those that worked against us.
My father said, why should it be?
Lithuania was always a country of law and order.
There was a court.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
There were lawyers.
He says, oh, no.
This is a different time.
It's time of war.
And those who sinned against us will definitely suffer, and maybe, many who did not do anything wrong may also suffer.
So I-- I suggest that you take your family and try to get to Russia.
My father said, it's so simple.
It's not so simple.
You can't get out.
